title: UN Innovation Training Library 2026: Explore Courses, Toolkits and Future-Ready Professional Skills
summary: The United Nations Innovation Network provides an extensive Innovation Training Library containing courses, toolkits, learning paths, professional certificates and other capacity-building resources developed or hosted by organizations across the United Nations system. The library is intended to help United Nations personnel and other interested learners build practical innovation capabilities. It allows users to explore new approaches, understand emerging technologies and consider how innovative methods can be applied within United Nations operations, public institutions, development programmes and other professional environments. Rather than operating as one course with a single application form, deadline or eligibility requirement, the UN Innovation Training Library functions as a searchable directory. Each listed resource has its own subject, duration, delivery format, intended audience and access arrangements. The library covers areas such as: Artificial intelligence and machine learning Generative artificial intelligence Data innovation and analytics Digital transformation Behavioural science Strategic foresight Human-centred design Innovation management Innovation scaling Sustainable and innovative finance Blockchain Automation and robotics Internet of Things Communications and knowledge sharing Entrepreneurship Climate action Digital health Digital agriculture Tourism innovation Urban development Aviation innovation Frontier technologies Important Clarification About the Number of Resources The training page currently displays a gallery counter showing 99 results. However, this number represents the total results appearing in the library at the time the page is viewed. It should not be interpreted as 99 conventional courses because the collection also contains: Toolkits Learning paths Professional certificate programmes Training compilations Fellowship programmes Accelerator programmes Mobile microlearning resources Other professional development materials For this reason, it is more accurate to describe the platform as a growing collection of innovation-related courses, toolkits and learning resources rather than stating that it provides a fixed number of courses. The library is also described as a living library, meaning that its content and total number of results can change when new resources are added or existing resources are revised or removed. What Is the UN Innovation Training Library? The UN Innovation Training Library is a curated collection of learning resources from across the United Nations system. Its purpose is to support the development of innovation capabilities among UN staff and other interested users. The official page states that the library can support both people who are new to innovation and professionals who are already leading teams or innovation-related activities. The library can be used by learners seeking to: Understand the fundamentals of innovation. Develop an innovation-oriented professional mindset. Learn how artificial intelligence can support organizational work. Understand the ethical and governance implications of AI. Improve data literacy, analysis and visualization. Use evidence more effectively in policymaking. Explore digital transformation in government and development. Apply behavioural science to public policy and programme design. Use human-centred design to improve products and services. Learn about sustainable finance and climate-related investment. Study emerging technologies such as blockchain and robotics. Improve training, facilitation and communication skills. Explore innovation in agriculture, health, tourism, aviation and cities. Connect professional learning with the Sustainable Development Goals. How the Training Library Is Organized The page contains filters that allow users to search according to development priorities, training providers, innovation subjects, time requirements and delivery methods. 1. Sustainable Development Goal Filters Users can search for resources related to humanitarian action or the Sustainable Development Goals. The available filters include: Humanitarian Action SDG 1: No Poverty SDG 2: Zero Hunger SDG 3: Good Health and Well-Being SDG 4: Quality Education SDG 5: Gender Equality SDG 6: Clean Water and Sanitation SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy SDG 8: Decent Work and Economic Growth SDG 9: Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure SDG 10: Reduced Inequalities SDG 11: Sustainable Cities and Communities SDG 12: Responsible Consumption and Production SDG 13: Climate Action SDG 14: Life Below Water SDG 15: Life on Land SDG 16: Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions SDG 17: Partnerships for the Goals These filters allow learners to connect their professional-development choices with specific development, humanitarian, environmental or governance priorities. 2. Training Provider Filters The library contains resources associated with numerous United Nations entities, specialized agencies and international institutions. Examples appearing in the provider filter include: Food and Agriculture Organization International Civil Aviation Organization International Labour Organization International Telecommunication Union United Nations Capital Development Fund United Nations Conference on Trade and Development United Nations Development Programme United Nations Environment Programme United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change United Nations Global Pulse United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees United Nations Human Settlements Programme United Nations Institute for Training and Research United Nations International Computing Centre United Nations System Staff College United Nations Tourism United Nations University UN Women UNICEF World Food Programme World Health Organization World Bank Group World Intellectual Property Organization World Meteorological Organization The complete filter contains many additional United Nations departments, offices and partner organizations. 3. Innovation Subject Filters Users can search the library according to the following innovation-related themes: 3D printing Artificial intelligence and machine learning Automation and robotics Behavioural science Blockchain Chatbots Communications and knowledge sharing Culture of innovation Data innovation Digital transformation Drones and satellite imagery Frontier technologies Generative AI Innovation in business processes Innovation in general, including human-centred design Innovation scaling Innovative finance Internet of Things Mobile phone-based innovations New business models Strategic foresight Virtual reality and augmented reality These categories demonstrate that the library addresses both technology-driven innovation and wider institutional or organizational change. 4. Time Requirements The library divides resources into four broad time categories: Short: A few hours Medium: A few days Long: A few weeks Very long: A few months The duration assigned to each listing is an indicative category rather than a detailed schedule. Users should check the individual programme page for the actual number of hours, days or weeks required. 5. Delivery Methods The available delivery modalities are: Online In-person Hybrid Most displayed resources are online, but the library also includes classroom-based and blended programmes. Major Learning Areas Available in the Library Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Artificial intelligence is one of the most prominent subject areas represented in the library. The available resources cover different levels of AI learning, including: Basic AI literacy Generative AI Large language models Prompt engineering AI governance AI ethics AI applications in health AI applications in hospitality AI for learning professionals AI for decision-makers AI and data storytelling AI skills for UN personnel AI Academy The AI Academy was developed and is operated by the UNICC AI Hub. It seeks to address the artificial-intelligence literacy gap across United Nations entities by offering a structured, multi-partner initiative aligned with UN 2.0 and the World Food Programme’s Data and AI Competency Framework. The library classifies it as a medium-duration online resource. Artificial Intelligence for Learning Professionals This UNITAR course examines how AI can be integrated into learning design without sacrificing empathy, human judgement and professional insight. It combines areas such as: Behavioural science Instructional design Strategic foresight Ethical AI Human-centred learning The programme is presented as a short online course. Artificial Intelligence Masterclass The Artificial Intelligence Masterclass explores the influence of AI on: Training Education Knowledge management Workforce development Everyday professional practices The course also examines emerging trends, ethical considerations and practical applications. Its description identifies students, educators and professionals as potential learners. AI Skills for UN Personnel AI Skills for UN Personnel is led by the United Nations System Staff College in collaboration with the Executive Office of the Secretary-General and Microsoft Elevate. It supports the UN 2.0 and UN80 agendas and is classified as a medium-duration hybrid programme. AI Essentials for Decision Makers This UNITAR programme is intended to help institutional leaders, government professionals and organizational decision-makers understand AI and lead digital transformation responsibly. It connects technical concepts with practical policy and management applications, including: Governance Strategic foresight Organizational performance Responsible digital transformation The library identifies it as a long online programme. UN Generative AI Professional Certificate The UN Generative AI Professional Certificate provides learning on the mechanisms and applications of generative AI in: Text Images Video It is listed as a long hybrid programme rather than a multi-month programme. Generative AI With Large Language Models This course provides more technical learning about the lifecycle of large language model applications. It addresses: Data gathering Model selection Transformer architecture Model training Fine-tuning Performance evaluation Deployment Scaling laws Compute requirements Inference Business opportunities and challenges It is categorized as a medium-duration online course. AI Governance, Ethics and Responsible Technology The library does not present AI only as a technical subject. Several listings focus on responsible governance and ethical use. These include: Artificial Intelligence Governance Toolkit AI Governance in Practice: Developing Secure and Innovative Frameworks Generative AI and Ethics: The Urgency of Now Ethics and Governance of Artificial Intelligence for Health Introduction to Artificial Intelligence and Women, Peace and Security Emerging Technologies and Human Rights 2026 Foundations of Humane Technology These resources address issues such as: Privacy Governance Regulation Security Human rights Gender responsiveness Conflict sensitivity Healthcare ethics Stakeholder engagement Organizational responsibility The Artificial Intelligence Governance Toolkit, for example, provides a framework for addressing privacy and governance at organizational and system levels. Data Analytics, Data Literacy and Data Storytelling The library contains several programmes for professionals who need to analyse, visualize, interpret and communicate data. E-Learning Path on Data Analytics The UN System Staff College’s E-Learning Path on Data Analytics is intended to strengthen participants’ ability to: Analyse data Visualize information Prepare reports Communicate evidence Learners complete modules and activities at their own pace while participating in online discussions and interacting with faculty and peers. It is listed as a long online learning path. UN Data Analytics Professional Certificate This programme combines: Self-paced modules Instructor-led sessions On-the-job practice Data analysis Data visualization Reporting Communication It is tailored to the UN context and is categorized as a very long online programme requiring several months. Introduction to Data Literacy This self-paced course introduces basic data literacy. It covers: Data input Data cleaning Data usability Interpretation Context Data storytelling It is classified as a short online course. Kamino 100 Series The Kamino 100 Series introduces the data science lifecycle, including: Data ingestion Data preparation Data visualization Machine learning Text analysis Storytelling The listing states that it is designed for learners regardless of their previous data-science knowledge or professional background. Digital Transformation and Digital Governance Digital transformation resources examine how institutions can design, manage and implement technology-enabled systems responsibly. Digital Fitness Programme The UNDP Digital Fitness Programme is a hands-on digital product design and management training programme. It is intended for operations and programme managers seeking to develop and roll out digital solutions. The library categorizes it as a medium-duration, in-person programme. Digital Health: Planning National Systems This WHO programme focuses on strengthening national digital-health governance and workforce capacity. It addresses: National digital-health strategies Digital architecture Standards Information infrastructure Emergency-preparedness technologies National planning Implementation capacity It is classified as a short online programme. Digital Agriculture The World Bank Group’s Digital Agriculture course examines digital agricultural technologies such as: Mobile applications Digital farmer identities Solar applications Portable agricultural devices Digital marketplaces Agricultural data platforms The description states that the course lasts five weeks and includes discussion forums and quizzes. However, the library’s broad duration filter labels it as medium, illustrating why users should check the detailed description rather than relying only on the general duration category. Digital4Sustainability Learning Path This learning path examines two major questions: How can digital technology support environmental and social sustainability? How can digital transformation itself become more sustainable? The programme is associated with UNEP and the United Nations System Staff College and is classified as a short online learning path. Digital Accessibility and Inclusion Relevant resources include: Web Accessibility: The Cornerstone of Digital Society Inclusive Digital Communication During Crisis and Emergency Situations Smart for All: Beyond Smart Cities Inclusive Digital Economies and Gender Equality Playbook These resources focus on making digital information, services and environments accessible to people regardless of gender, age, disability, ability or context. Innovation Management and Human-Centred Design Innovation Management The UNICEF Innovation Management course is described as a nine-week online programme. It explores: Innovation concepts Idea generation Selection of ideas Strategy formulation Implementation New products and services Business models Social networks Innovation-project tools The course is designed to help learners develop an innovative mindset and understand how organizations convert ideas into practical outputs. Managing Innovation The UNIDO Managing Innovation course supports structured innovation within organizations, particularly small and medium-sized enterprises. The programme is promoted in partnership with the International Organization for Standardization and is listed as a short online course. Human-Centred Design and Service Design The World Food Programme course introduces human-centred design and service design. It explains: Why users should remain at the centre of services How service design supports technology development Why human-centred design is valuable How the approach can be applied in large organizations It is categorized as a short online course. Innovation for Impact Learning Path The UN System Staff College’s Innovation for Impact Learning Path supports capacity development across the UN system. It includes: Introduction to Innovation Learning Module UN Innovation Toolkit Learning Module Digital tools Innovation frameworks Practical innovation approaches The programme aims to improve impact and strengthen the future resilience of the UN workforce. UN Innovation Toolkit The UN Innovation Toolkit is a practical resource for supporting innovation across the United Nations system. Because it is a toolkit rather than a conventional course, it is one of the reasons the library’s result total should not be described entirely as a number of courses. Behavioural Science and Decision-Making Introduction to Behavioral Science in Public Policy This World Bank Group course introduces the role of behavioural science in policy design. It examines applications in: Education Healthcare Social norms Gender equality Tax compliance Climate action Other public-policy areas The library lists it as a short online course. Social and Behaviour Change Theory and Practice This UNICEF resource introduces social and behaviour change approaches used to generate measurable improvements in programmes and communities. It emphasizes: Evidence Participation Community engagement Human rights Behavioural outcomes Co-design Systems Change and Behavioural Science for Climate Action This course was developed by the UNFCCC Youth4Capacity programme with support from the Italian Ministry of Environment and Energy Security and the United Nations System Staff College. It helps young leaders use: Systems thinking Behavioural science Complex-problem analysis Behavioural interventions Scalable climate solutions The library classifies it as a short online programme. Sustainable Finance, Climate Action and Environmental Innovation SDG Finance Academy E-Learning Programme The UNDP SDG Finance Academy provides self-paced modules covering different areas of sustainable finance. The programme is intended to help learners understand: Sustainable-finance concepts Financial tools Resource-mobilization strategies Financing for the Sustainable Development Goals It is listed as a short online programme connected to SDG 17. Introduction to Sustainable Finance for Climate and Energy This UNDP course is described as a free online introduction to climate and energy finance. It covers: Green bonds Blue bonds Debt-for-nature swaps Carbon markets Private-finance solutions Integrated National Financing Frameworks Impact measurement and management Investment de-risking The course is connected to SDGs 7, 13 and 17. Introduction to Impact Measurement and Management and Finance Tracking This resource introduces: Sustainability impact measurement Impact management Finance tracking Taxonomies Reporting systems Data frameworks SDG-aligned decision-making It is classified as a short online course. Nature-Based Solutions for Disaster and Climate Resilience This UNEP course explains how nature-based solutions can strengthen resilience to climate change and disasters. Participants learn about: Connections between human activity and ecological systems Disaster-risk reduction Climate resilience Ecosystem-based approaches Nature-based planning tools It is listed as a short online course linked to SDG 13. Electronic Waste Training The library includes ITU resources addressing: Global electronic-waste trends E-waste statistics Relevant legislation Data collection Environmental implications International monitoring One listed course, Addressing the E-Waste Challenge Through Data and Statistics, is categorized as a short online data-innovation course connected to climate action. Entrepreneurship and Business Innovation Innovating for Success: A Guide for Entrepreneurs This course helps aspiring young entrepreneurs understand: Entrepreneurship pathways Business risks and rewards Business start-up processes Entrepreneurial mindset Opportunity identification Innovation It is categorized as a short online programme linked to decent work and economic growth. Digital Business Innovations for Women Entrepreneurs and Managers This UNIDO course is intended to help women develop the digital knowledge and skills needed to operate and manage businesses in the Industry 4.0 environment. It is listed as a short online digital-transformation course. Green and Digital Entrepreneurship for Women The library also includes a programme addressing: Green business Circular economy Digital entrepreneurship Sustainable business operations Green supply chains Green finance Women’s economic empowerment Sector-Specific Innovation Training Agriculture and Food Systems Relevant resources include: FAO Science and Innovation Strategy Digital Agriculture Tropical Agriculture Platform Human-Centred Design and Service Design in WFP The Tropical Agriculture Platform resource contains six lessons covering: Agricultural innovation systems Capacity development Capacity-needs assessment at innovation-niche level Capacity-needs assessment at organizational level Enabling environments Monitoring, evaluation and learning Each lesson is described as requiring approximately 30 to 40 minutes. Health Health-related resources include: Digital Health: Planning National Systems Ethics and Governance of Artificial Intelligence for Health Building the Digital Economy: Pathways in Healthcare and Government These programmes examine digital-health planning, AI implementation, ethics, governance and the particular needs of developing countries. Tourism and Hospitality Tourism-related resources include: Tourism Innovation Artificial Intelligence in Hospitality: Challenges and Business Opportunities Innovation and Digital Transformation in the Tourism Industry Tourism Innovation is a four-week online course delivered by Externado University of Colombia. It covers tourism guidelines, legal frameworks, value chains and sustainable practices. Participants must obtain an 80 per cent score on the final quiz to earn a certificate. Aviation Aviation-related listings include: Innovation in Aviation Training Programme Innovation in Aviation: Fundamentals Overview of Unmanned Aviation Fundamentals These resources cover innovation concepts, regulatory challenges, organizational adaptation and unmanned aviation. Cities and Urban Mobility The library includes programmes on: Sustainable urban mobility Transit-oriented development Low-carbon transport Active mobility Inclusive transportation Digital governance in African cities Digitally accessible urban environments Blockchain, Robotics and Other Frontier Technologies Blockchain Training The library includes: The Use of Blockchain in Trade Facilitation Introduction to Blockchain for Trade Blockchain for Government These courses examine blockchain in: International trade Supply chains Digitalization Automation Government systems Public administration Blockchain for Government is specifically described as a programme helping government officials understand and use blockchain technology in public administration. Humanoid Robots at Work This ITCILO resource examines AI-powered humanoid and semi-humanoid robots. It considers: Human-machine collaboration Changes in employment Learning and education Risks Opportunities Ethics Decent work Social justice The programme is framed around the relationship between emerging technology and the future of work. Quantum for Everyone Quantum for Everyone is described as a free online course covering the basic building blocks of quantum technology. It is listed as a short online frontier-technology course. Frontier ICTs for Sustainable Development This ESCAP course examines: Artificial intelligence Blockchain Internet of Things Policy risks Good practices International cooperation Sustainable development It is particularly relevant to digital leaders and policymakers in developing countries. UN 2.0 Learning Resources The UN 2.0 learning resources focus on five cross-cutting competence areas known as the Quintet of Change: Innovation Strategic foresight Behavioural science Data Digital transformation UN 2.0 Fundamentals E-Learning Path The United Nations System Staff College developed five freely available, self-paced introductory modules covering the Quintet of Change. The learning path is listed as a long online resource. UN 2.0 Learning Path: The Quintet of Change The UNICEF learning path begins with an introduction to UN 2.0 and then explores the five competence areas. It includes: LinkedIn courses Advanced learning paths UN policies UN strategies Practical tools UNICEF learning resources It is categorized as a short online learning path. Are the Training Resources Free? The library does not provide one universal fee policy that applies to every listing. Some individual descriptions expressly state that their courses or resources are free. Examples include: Elements of AI Introduction to Sustainable Finance for Climate and Energy Quantum for Everyone Micro Training of Trainers UN 2.0 Fundamentals E-Learning Path Google Cloud Generative AI Training Resources OpenAI Explained Other listings do not state their fees directly on the library page. Prospective learners should therefore open the individual programme page and confirm: Registration fees Tuition charges Certificate fees Institutional-access requirements Nomination requirements Travel costs for in-person programmes Technology requirements Current availability Are Certificates Available? There is no universal certificate policy for the entire library. Some listings are specifically structured as professional certificate programmes, including: UN Data Analytics Professional Certificate UN Generative AI Professional Certificate Other courses may award a certificate after completion or assessment. For example, the Tourism Innovation course requires participants to achieve an 80 per cent score on the final quiz. Learners should verify the following on the individual provider’s page: Whether a certificate is offered Whether the certificate is free or paid Attendance requirements Assessment requirements Minimum passing score Assignment requirements Certificate-verification arrangements Application Deadline There is no single deadline for the entire UN Innovation Training Library. It is a collection of independently managed resources, so application dates and registration periods vary. How to Access a Training Resource There is no single application form for the entire library. Users should follow these steps: Visit the UN Innovation Training Library. Review the available resource gallery. Select a relevant Sustainable Development Goal if applicable. Filter by UN entity or provider. Choose an innovation subject. Select the preferred time requirement. Choose online, in-person or hybrid delivery. Open the selected listing. Read its complete description. Follow the link to the original provider. Confirm that the programme is still available. Review eligibility requirements. Check the deadline. Verify the fee and certificate conditions. Complete registration through the provider’s official platform. Who Should Explore the Library? The library may be useful for: United Nations personnel Government officials Policymakers Public administrators Development practitioners Humanitarian professionals Monitoring and evaluation specialists Data analysts Information management professionals Researchers Students Educators Trainers Facilitators Technology professionals Digital-transformation teams Sustainability professionals Climate-action practitioners Entrepreneurs Business managers Healthcare professionals Agriculture professionals Aviation professionals Tourism professionals Innovation managers Organizational leaders However, inclusion in this general audience does not mean that every person is eligible for every listed programme. Some resources are designed for the general public, while others target specific groups such as: UN personnel Government policymakers Women entrepreneurs Young leaders Developing-country officials Trainers and facilitators UN-led innovation teams Professionals working in particular sectors Important Points to Verify Before Enrolling Before registering for any resource, users should verify: Whether the programme is currently open. Whether it is a course, toolkit, fellowship or certificate. Whether participation is open to the public. Whether UN employment is required. Whether nomination is required. Whether there is a fee. Whether a certificate is provided. Whether the course is fully online. Whether live attendance is required. Whether the programme has regional restrictions. Whether it requires previous technical knowledge. Whether its duration remains accurate. Whether the linked external page is active. Whether a stated intake date has already passed. Frequently Asked Questions Is the UN Innovation Training Library a single programme? No. It is a searchable collection of independent courses, toolkits, learning paths, professional certificates and related resources. Does the library contain only courses? No. The gallery also contains toolkits, fellowships, accelerator programmes, certificate programmes and other professional-learning resources. Why should the number of courses not be placed in the headline? The webpage’s result counter reflects the total number of gallery entries at a particular time. It does not mean that every result is a conventional course, and the total may change because the platform is a living library. Are all resources open to the general public? No universal access rule is stated. Some are open broadly, while others are intended for UN personnel, government officials, women, young leaders or other specific audiences. Are all courses free? No universal fee policy is provided. Some resources are expressly described as free, while the costs of others must be checked on their respective provider pages. Is there one application deadline? No. Each independently managed programme may have its own deadline or registration status. Are certificates available? Certificates are available through selected programmes, but certificate requirements differ. Are the resources online? Many are online, while others are in-person or hybrid. How long do the programmes take? The library classifies resources as short, medium, long or very long, ranging from a few hours to several months. Conclusion The UN Innovation Training Library is a broad professional-development resource for people seeking to build knowledge and practical capabilities in innovation, artificial intelligence, data, digital transformation, behavioural science, sustainable finance and emerging technologies. Its strength lies in the variety of learning formats represented in the library. Users can find short introductory resources, detailed online courses, in-person training, hybrid programmes, professional certificates, toolkits, learning paths, fellowships and accelerator opportunities. The platform should not be presented as a single application opportunity or as a fixed collection of a particular number of courses. It is more accurately described as a living library of courses, toolkits and professional learning resources from across the United Nations system. There is no general deadline, fee, certificate policy or eligibility requirement applying to all listings. Each resource must be reviewed individually to determine its current status, intended audience, application procedure, cost, duration and certificate conditions. Prospective learners should use the library’s filters to identify relevant resources and then verify all final details through the official page of the individual training provider. For more global opportunities, visit HERE . The post UN Innovation Training Library 2026: Explore Courses, Toolkits and Future-Ready Professional Skills appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Wilson Center Research Fellowship Program 2027–2028 (Fully Funded): Receive USD 120,000 to Conduct Policy Research
summary: Applications are now open for the Wilson Center Research Fellowship Program 2027–2028 , a prestigious fully funded 12-month research fellowship for accomplished scholars, policy practitioners, journalists, and public intellectuals worldwide. The fellowship provides a monthly stipend of USD 10,000 (USD 120,000 total) , remote research opportunities, funded travel to Washington, D.C. , and direct engagement with policymakers on pressing global issues. The Wilson Center will award 10–20 fellowships for the 2027–2028 cohort to individuals conducting nonpartisan, policy-relevant research addressing major foreign policy and global challenges. Fellowship Summary Host Institution: Wilson Center (Woodrow Wilson International Center for Scholars) Location: Remote (with funded visits to Washington, D.C., USA) Fellowship Duration: June 1, 2027 – May 30, 2028 (12 months) Number of Fellowships: 10–20 Funding: USD 10,000 per month (USD 120,000 total) Application Deadline: 30 September 2026 (11:59 PM ET) Wilson Center Research Fellowship Program The Wilson Center invites scholars, practitioners, journalists, and public intellectuals to take part in its flagship Research Fellowship Program. Fellows conduct research and write in their areas of interest, while interacting with policymakers in Washington and other fellows. The Center accepts policy-relevant, nonpartisan fellowship proposals that address key foreign policy challenges confronting the United States and the world. About the Center The Woodrow Wilson International Center for Scholars aims to unite the world of ideas to the world of policy by supporting pre-eminent scholarship and linking that scholarship to issues of concern to policymakers in Washington. The Wilson Center promotes nonpartisan research and evidence-based scholarship that seeks to inform, but not advocate for a particular political viewpoint. Congress established the Center in 1968 as the official, national memorial to President Wilson. Unlike the physical monuments in the nation s capital, it is a living memorial whose work and scholarship commemorate “the ideals and concerns of Woodrow Wilson.” As both a distinguished scholar and national leader, President Wilson felt strongly that the scholar and the policymaker were “engaged in a common enterprise.” Today, the Center takes seriously the need to bridge the gap between the world of ideas and public policy, bringing them into creative contact, enriching the work of both, and enabling each to learn from the other. This continuing dialogue between public policy and scholarship makes the Center unique. Details of the 2027-2028 Fellowship The Wilson Center invites scholars, practitioners, journalists, and public intellectuals to apply for its flagship Research Fellowship competition . The Center will award 10 to 20 fellowships for the 2027-2028 class. Fellows are encouraged to interact with policymakers in Washington, D.C. and other scholars who are working on similar research and topics. Stipend and Duration The fellowship will commence on June 1, 2027 and end on May 30, 2028. The Center offers a stipend of $10,000 per month for a twelve-month fellowship. Conditions of Award Fellows will work remotely during their tenure with the Wilson Center. The program will include a trip to Washington D.C. at the start and end of the fellowship, to be paid for by the Wilson Center. The focus of each fellow’s tenure will be their research project. The project must be a policy-relevant, nonpartisan project that addresses key challenges confronting the United States and the world. The project must align with at least one of the four priority areas for the Wilson Center. Refer to the Applicant Eligibility section page for additional information regarding the fellowship projects. Fellows are required to complete their project as proposed. They are also required to give a Work in Progress presentation, which is an internal meeting where fellows can speak about their work, share ideas, and receive feedback from their peers. Fellows will attend the Work in Progress presentations given by their colleagues. Fellows will also be expected to actively contribute to the goals and mission of the Wilson Center during their tenure. The Center will work with all fellows to share their expertise with the Washington policy community. These interactions may range from a deep background briefing for a federal agency to an informal roundtable discussion with members of Congress and their staffs. They may deliver more formal presentations to the public, including colloquia, seminars, workshops, or other coordinated events with the Center. Fellows will also be encouraged to produce policy reports, op-eds, and other short written and audio-visual products during their tenure and engage with the broader Washington community of scholars working on similar topics. The Center will provide guidance for all fellows on translating academic research into policy-relevant content. Deadline for Applications Fellowship applications, including letters of recommendation, must be submitted online by 11:59 pm ET on September 30, 2026 . Applicants will be notified of the results of the selection process by early March 2027. Applicant Eligibility Eligibility Requirements for Applicants Applicants must meet the following criteria to be eligible for a fellowship: Applicants from academia must have a PhD in hand and have published a book or monograph beyond the PhD dissertation, or a substantial body of research articles (5+) in reputable peer-reviewed journals. Applicants from government, policy, or the nonprofit sector must have achieved a level of professional distinction and experience equivalent to advanced academic standing. This is typically at least 10 years of relevant experience and a record of substantial policy impact, publication, or leadership in their field. Applicants must be proficient in English, as the Center is designed to encourage the exchange of ideas among its fellows and provide educational opportunities to Congress. You do not need an institutional affiliation to apply. Scholars and practitioners who within the last 5 years were awarded a Wilson Center International Competition Fellowship or Research Fellowship should not apply. Scholars and practitioners who have received other categories of research awards or fellowships from the Wilson Center are not precluded from applying for a Research Fellowship. However, the nature and recency of the prior award may be among the factors considered during the selection process. Please note that this is not a residential fellowship. For any travel to the United States for associated events or meetings, fellows are expected to hold a valid passport and obtain the appropriate visa. Fellows may take leave from their current positions or maintain additional affiliations during their Wilson Center Research Fellowship. Applicants will be required to describe how they will balance other responsibilities while actively contributing to the goals and mission of the Wilson Center. Eligibility Requirements for Project Proposals Project proposals must meet the following criteria to be considered: Projects must be nonpartisan in nature. Projects must focus on at least one of the following scholarship pillars of the Wilson Center : strategic competition, economic statecraft, technology and innovation, and regional scholarship. Primary project outputs may include but are not limited to a book manuscript, series of long-form articles, or a briefing roundtable series for policymakers. Additional projects demonstrating a high-level of scholarship may be considered. Primary project outputs may not include rewriting or completion of doctoral dissertations; projects in the visual arts, dance, or musical composition; editing of texts, papers, and documents; or preparations of textbooks, anthologies, translations, or memoirs. Selection Process Applications that satisfy the eligibility requirements are subsequently subjected to a multi-stage review process involving evaluations by Wilson Center experts and external specialists with relevant expertise. Final decisions for all federally-funded Research Fellowships are contingent on the approval of the Center’s Board of Trustees and subject to available funding. The basic criteria for selection are: Significance of the proposed research, including the importance and originality of the project. Explain the urgency and importance of your work to resolve a larger problem in foreign policy. The relevance of the project to contemporary policy issues and policymakers, with the aim of bridging the worlds of scholarship and policy. Quality of the proposal in definition, organization, clarity, and scope. Describe what the reviewers will learn from your project, why it is important, and how the reviewer will know your conclusions are valid. Capabilities and achievements of the applicant and their propensity to accomplish the proposed project. The proposal should convince the reviewer that something is genuinely at stake with your inquiry and your project will yield interesting results. Potential of a candidate to actively contribute to the priorities and mission of the Center by making expert research accessible to policymakers and a broader audience. The Center accepts policy-relevant, non-advocacy fellowship proposals that will address key challenges confronting the United States and the world. The Center encourages projects that transcend narrow specialties to connect to broader policy and global issues. Fellows should want to, and be prepared to, interact with policymakers in Washington, other Wilson Center fellows, and scholars within DC and the world working on similar issues. It is essential that projects have relevance to public policy and at least one of the four pillars of scholarship of the Wilson Center: strategic competition, economic statecraft, technology and innovation, and regional scholarship. Projects should involve fresh research in terms of both the overall field and the author’s previous work. The Application A complete application must include the following: The Fellowship Application Form. A current CV that does not exceed 3 pages, using 12-point font . A project proposal that does not exceed 5 pages, using 12-point font. 2 letters of reference. All materials must be submitted in English. Applications must be submitted online through Interfolio by 11:59 pm ET on September 30, 2026. Additional details about each section of the application are included below. Fellowship Application Form When submitting your application through Interfolio, you must complete the Fellowship Application Form to be considered for a Wilson Center Research Fellowship. The form will request general details regarding your contact information, educational background, professional experience, and research project title. There is also space for applicants to share how they plan to engage with the Wilson Center community and to disclose any potential conflicts of interest. Additionally, applicants should describe any other affiliations they intend to maintain during their fellowship year. The Application Form fields are provided below for reference purposes only. The form must be completed electronically and submitted through the Interfolio application portal. The Wilson Center will not accept forms attached as PDFs, Word documents, or other external file formats. General Questions Full Name (First and Last)* Email* Phone Number* Current Address* Date of Birth (MM/DD/YYYY)* Gender* Primary Citizenship* Additional Citizenship Professional Background Professional Title* Professional Affiliation* Scholarly Discipline* Highest Level of Education* Institute and Location of Highest Level of Education* Major Field of Highest Level of Education* PhD Dissertation (if applicable) Years of Professional Experience Wilson Center Community Title of Proposed Wilson Center Research Project* How do you intend to regularly engage with and contribute to the Wilson Center community during your fellowship? (max 200 words)* Do you intend to hold any other positions or affiliations during your tenure as a Wilson Center Research Fellow? If yes, how will you balance those responsibilities with the expectations of the Wilson Center fellowship program?* Have you ever had any formal affiliation with the Wilson Center, whether as staff, fellow, intern, contractor, or other? If yes, please explain the nature and recency of the affiliation.* Conflicts of Interest Are you currently or have you ever been a registered lobbyist in the United States?* Are you currently or have you ever registered under the Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA)?* Do you have a former or current affiliation with a non-US government agency, military, or government sponsored organization? If yes, please explain the nature and recency of the affiliation.* Bearing in mind that the Wilson Center is a Congressionally chartered, official U.S. presidential memorial, please disclose any potential conflicts of interest that could arise should you be awarded a Wilson Center Research Fellowship.* APPLY NOW Your CV Your CV must be included as part of your application. It cannot exceed 3 pages, using 12-point font. For any publications, please include exact titles, names of publishers, dates of publication, and status of forthcoming publications. Feel free to include an explanation for lapses in your CV and any opportunities you were offered but unable to accept or were rescinded due to unforeseen circumstances. We understand that these things do occur and that not every CV will look the same. Project Proposal The project proposal is the heart of your application. It cannot exceed 5 pages, using 12-point font. It is essential to make your project clear to individuals outside your own field and to explain its broader implications. The following elements should be addressed in the proposal: Detailed description of the topic and its importance. Original contribution of the proposed research. Basic ideas and hypotheses. Methodology to be used, including the activities you will undertake to gather the data you need for your project and the techniques that you will use to analyze the data in order to prove your thesis. The present status of your research, including how much has already been done in relevant collections and archives, and what you would hope to accomplish at the Center. Why you chose the Wilson Center for your project and how the project upholds Wilson Center’s commitment to nonpartisanship. How the project aligns to one or more areas of scholarship (geostrategic competition, economic statecraft, technology and innovation, and regional scholarship). Relevance of the project to contemporary policy issues and policymakers in Washington, D.C. and how the research will be communicated or made accessible to those audiences. A brief bibliography, including primary and relevant secondary sources. Letters of Recommendation Two letters of recommendation must be submitted online by 11:59 pm ET on September 30, 2026. Only two letters will be accepted. When completing the Request a Recommendation field in Interfolio, please ensure you enter the correct email address for each referee. It is your responsibility to ensure your reference letters are received; applications missing these letters will be considered incomplete. Your referees should be familiar with you and your work. We recommend sending them a copy of your project description so they can comment specifically on: Your proposed study and your qualifications for undertaking it. How you and your work would contribute to the Center’s goal of bridging the gap between the world of learning and the world of public affairs. All letters must be written in English and should follow the referee guidelines provided below. Applicants are strongly encouraged to follow up with their referees to confirm they received the Interfolio email prompt and that they submit their letters through the portal prior to the application deadline. Guidelines for Referees In your letter of reference, please include an assessment of the applicant and the proposed project. The Center awards fellowships to individuals with outstanding project proposals in a broad range of the social sciences and humanities on international issues. Topics should intersect with questions of public policy. You should judge the application according to the following criteria: Significance of the proposed research, including the importance and originality of the project. The relevance of the project to contemporary policy issues and policymakers, with the aim of bridging the worlds of scholarship and policy. Quality of the proposal in definition, organization, clarity, and scope. Capabilities and achievements of the applicant and their propensity to accomplish the proposed project. Potential of a candidate to actively contribute to the priorities and mission of the Center by making expert research accessible to policymakers and a broader audience. Frequently Asked Questions Please read the Frequently Asked Questions below for additional information regarding the Fellowship Program. Applicants may address additional inquiries to fellowships@wilsoncenter.org. Fellowship Eligibility Who is eligible to apply? Scholars, policy practitioners, journalists, and public intellectuals with project proposals on global issues are invited to apply. Topics and scholarship should relate to key public policy challenges and align with at least one of the following pillars of the Wilson Center: strategic competition, economic statecraft, technology and innovation, and regional issues. Academic applicants must have a doctorate or JD. For other applicants, an equivalent level of professional achievement is expected. I am not a U.S. citizen. May I apply? This is a non-residential fellowship with no citizenship limitations. However, fellows are expected to travel to Washington, D.C. for two visits during the fellowship; you must hold a valid passport and be able to obtain the appropriate visa. Please note that applicants currently living in the United States must be legal permanent residents or U.S. citizens at the time of application. The Wilson Center is unable to sponsor J-1 visas. I am retired and do not have an institutional affiliation. May I apply? Yes. You do not need an active institutional affiliation to apply. I have completed all requirements for my Ph.D. except for my dissertation. May I apply for a fellowship? No. Academics must be at the postdoctoral level and have published a book or monograph beyond the Ph.D. dissertation, or a substantial body of research articles (typically five or more) in reputable peer-reviewed journals. Applicants outside of academia should have an equivalent level of professional achievement. I have a Ph.D. but have not published a book yet. Is it worth applying? For most academic candidates, a published book or monograph is required. However, a substantial body of research (typically five or more articles) in reputable, peer-reviewed journals may substitute for a book. Exceptions to the book requirement are made for applicants from fields like law, where scholarly output is traditionally comprised of articles rather than monographs, and practitioners who are pursuing or obtained a Ph.D. later in their career. Are there age limits for eligibility? There are no age limits for eligibility. However, all applicants should present evidence of their ability to carry out the proposed project, such as publications and other relevant experience. I was previously awarded a Wilson Center Competition Fellowship. May I apply again? Within the last five years: Scholars and practitioners who were awarded a Wilson Center International Competition Fellowship or Research Fellowship within the past five years are not eligible to apply. More than five years ago: Alumni who received a Wilson Center International Competition Fellowship or Research Fellowship more than five years ago are welcome to reapply. Other award categories: Applicants who have received other types of research awards or fellowships from the Wilson Center are also eligible to apply for a Research Fellowship. Please note that the nature and recency of the prior award may be among the factors considered during the selection process. The Application and Proposals Where can I find the online application? Please visit the Fellowship Application Form here What happens if I receive an error when applying online? Please contact help@interfolio.com for any technical support with the Interfolio platform. You may also contact the Wilson Center at fellowships@wilsoncenter.org for additional support. Is the Center looking for particular topics this year? The Center welcomes all applications that are nonpartisan in nature, policy relevant, and demonstrate alignment with one or more of the following scholarship pillars: strategic competition, economic statecraft, technology and innovation, and regional issues. It is very important to explain the relevance of your project to contemporary policy issues. Are applicants allowed to apply jointly or as collaborators? No. Wilson Center fellowships are non-collaborative awards, and applicants must apply as sole investigators. The Center evaluates proposals based on the individual scholar’s project, qualifications, and capacity to complete the work independently. Applicants may certainly be involved in larger research agendas outside the fellowship, but the proposal submitted to the Wilson Center must represent an individual, stand-alone project; led and written by the applicant; and independent of any co-authored or jointly executed research efforts. Can Center staff look over my proposal before I submit it? No. Unfortunately, Center staff are not able to read or comment on draft proposals. It is important to maintain a fair and impartial process. The Center recommends that you show your proposal to colleagues who are knowledgeable in your field so that they may make constructive comments. What do you mean by topics and scholarship should relate to key public policy challenges? The Center aims to unite the world of ideas to the world of policy by supporting preeminent nonpartisan scholarship and linking that scholarship to issues of concern to officials in Washington. The Center distinguishes itself from policy institutions in its consideration of issues in a broader and more reflective manner beyond a narrow analysis of current crises and policy options. Applicants should think in terms of what they have to say to policymakers in Washington and how they can inform policy decisions through their work. I have received my Ph.D. and would like to come to the Center to continue working toward publication of my dissertation manuscript. Is this an eligible proposal? No. Your project must be a separate project from your dissertation. Your application should be new research. May I ask to see an example of an awarded proposal? No. The Center does not provide examples of awarded proposals because each application is reviewed on an individual basis according to the criteria for selection in the application guidelines. If I am in the D.C. area, may I come in for an interview? No. The Center does not conduct fellowship interviews. You require two letters of reference. May I send additional references? The Center requires two letters of reference. No additional letters will be accepted. How will I know that you have received my application online? The Center requires two letters of reference. No additional letters will be accepted. How do I know the Center has received my reference letters? The application form will allow you to track reference letter submissions. It is up to the applicant to follow up with their referees to confirm that they have sent their letters to the Center through the online system. I applied for a fellowship last year and did not get one. If I reapply this year with the same proposal, can I use the same letters of reference? No. The Center requires that your referees submit updated letters of reference with current dates to accompany your application. However, you may ask the same individual from past applications to serve as your referee. The Selection Process and Logistics What is the selection process? Applications that meet all eligibility requirements undergo a comprehensive, multi-stage review process. This includes internal evaluations by Wilson Center experts, followed by external evaluations conducted by a panel of peer specialists with relevant expertise. Final fellowship awards are contingent upon the approval of the Fellowship Committee of the Center s Board of Trustees and are subject to available funding . When will I know if I have received a fellowship? All selected and non-selected applicants will be notified in March 2027. When do I start my Fellowship? If awarded a fellowship, your tenure will begin on June 1, 2027 and end on May 30, 2028. What are the residency requirements? Fellows will work remotely for the tenure of their appointment. They will be required to travel to Washington, D.C. at the beginning and end of the program. Fellows may be invited to participate in additional Wilson Center programs throughout the year. Travel and lodging will be arranged by the Wilson Center. Does the Center pay for fellows’ travel? The Center will arrange for travel to Washington, D.C. at the beginning and end of the program. The Center will not pay for short-term research trips a fellow may wish to take during their tenure. How are stipends awarded? Money for stipends comes from allocated federal funds. The Center offers a stipend of $10,000 a month for the duration of the twelve-month fellowship. Fellows will receive their stipends on a monthly basis. Does the Center offer health insurance? No. Fellows are responsible for their own health insurance. Will I be offered research assistance as a fellow? The Center intends to provide part-time research assistants during the fall and spring semesters. However, this is contingent upon available funding and our ability to match your specific project with an assistant who has the appropriate skills and experience. title: Australia COP31 2026 Opportunities: Submit Australian Pavilion Event Proposals or Apply for Climate Conference Travel Support to Attend the UN Climate Summit in Türkiye
summary: As Australia prepares to play a historic leadership role at the 31st United Nations Climate Change Conference (COP31), individuals, organizations, community groups, researchers, youth leaders, First Nations representatives, businesses, and civil society organizations now have exciting opportunities to participate in one of the world s most influential climate events. The Australian Government, through the Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water (DCCEEW), has opened applications for two major COP31 engagement opportunities: COP31 Australian Pavilion Event Proposals COP31 Climate Conference Travel Support Program These initiatives are designed to ensure Australia s participation at COP31 reflects the country s innovation, diversity, clean energy transition, and commitment to global climate action. COP31 will take place in Antalya, Türkiye , from 9 to 20 November 2026 , where Australia will serve as the President of Negotiations while partnering with Türkiye and Pacific nations to deliver one of the world s largest international climate conferences. Whether you want to organize an event at Australia s official COP31 Pavilion or seek financial support to attend the conference, these opportunities provide a valuable platform to contribute to global climate discussions and showcase Australian leadership. About COP31 The Conference of the Parties (COP) is the world s premier annual climate summit under the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC). Each year, COP brings together: National governments International organizations Researchers Businesses Investors Civil society Indigenous communities Youth representatives Climate advocates Environmental organizations The conference serves as the primary international forum for negotiating and implementing global climate policies, including commitments under the Paris Agreement. COP31 represents an especially significant milestone for Australia because the country will play a central role in facilitating negotiations, encouraging stronger climate ambition, and supporting implementation of international climate commitments. The event will also provide Australia with an opportunity to showcase national achievements in clean energy, innovation, sustainability, and regional partnerships. Australia s Leadership Role at COP31 Australia s role extends far beyond participating as a delegation. As President of Negotiations , Australia will: Guide international climate negotiations Help facilitate consensus among participating countries Promote ambitious climate action Support implementation of the Paris Agreement Showcase Australia s climate leadership Strengthen partnerships with Türkiye and Pacific nations Highlight Australia s clean energy transition The conference also presents an opportunity to demonstrate Australia s progress in renewable energy, electrification, and sustainable development. Opportunity 1: COP31 Australian Pavilion Event Proposals One of the key opportunities currently open is the submission of proposals for events to be featured within the Australian Pavilion during COP31. What is the Australian Pavilion? Every year at COP, participating countries establish national pavilions that serve as spaces for: Panel discussions Workshops Presentations Policy dialogues Networking events Knowledge sharing Showcasing innovative projects Demonstrating climate leadership The Australian Pavilion acts as Australia s public platform for presenting its climate initiatives to an international audience. For COP31, the Pavilion will place particular emphasis on: Clean energy transition Electrification Benefits of clean energy for households Australia s partnership with Pacific nations Climate innovation Practical climate solutions Click HERE to learn more. What Can Applicants Propose? Organizations are invited to submit proposals for activities such as: Public events Panel discussions Workshops Presentations Interactive sessions Knowledge-sharing activities Climate-focused content Collaborative discussions Each proposal should demonstrate how it contributes to Australia s climate story and supports the overall objectives of COP31. Click HERE to register. Submission Guidelines Applicants should note the following: Each proposed event requires a separate application form. Organizations are encouraged to nominate one primary contact person to coordinate submissions. Full submission details are available through the COP Engagement Hub. Deadline for Pavilion Event Proposals Application Deadline: Friday, 31 July 2026 Organizations are encouraged to prepare their proposals well before the closing date. Opportunity 2: COP31 Climate Conference Travel Support Program The Australian Government has also launched the COP31 Climate Conference Travel Support Program , providing financial assistance to enable a diverse range of Australians to attend the conference in person. The initiative aims to increase participation from individuals and communities that are traditionally underrepresented in international climate negotiations. Purpose of the Travel Support Program The programme seeks to ensure Australia s delegation reflects the country s diversity by supporting representatives from different backgrounds, sectors, and regions. Rather than limiting participation to established organizations, the programme encourages broader community representation. Who Is Encouraged to Apply? Applications are welcomed from individuals and organizations across Australia, including: Young people aged 18–35 First Nations Australians Regional communities Community organizations Climate advocates Environmental organizations Researchers Businesses Civil society organizations Industry representatives Youth organizations Community leaders The programme particularly encourages applications from groups that have historically been underrepresented within the COP process. What Does the Programme Support? Successful applicants will receive travel support that enables participation in COP31. Applicants should carefully review the official Grant Opportunity Guidelines for detailed eligibility requirements and funding conditions. Click HERE to learn more. Assessment Process Applications will be evaluated according to the eligibility and assessment criteria outlined in the official grant documentation. Prospective applicants should ensure they: Meet all eligibility requirements Address every assessment criterion Submit complete supporting documentation Apply before the closing date Click HERE to register. Deadline for Travel Support Applications Application Deadline: Monday, 27 July 2026 Late applications will not be considered. Why These Opportunities Matter Participation in COP31 provides significant professional and organizational benefits. Successful participants may have opportunities to: Participate in one of the world s largest climate conferences Showcase Australian innovation internationally Build partnerships with global organizations Engage directly with policymakers Network with climate experts Contribute to implementation of the Paris Agreement Share successful Australian initiatives Strengthen Australia s international climate profile Promote clean energy solutions Engage with global sustainability leaders Australia s Focus Areas at COP31 Australia intends to highlight several priority areas during the conference, including: Clean energy transition Electrification Renewable energy Household energy transformation Climate innovation Regional cooperation Pacific partnerships Practical climate solutions Sustainable development International collaboration How to Prepare a Strong Application Applicants seeking to maximize their chances of success should consider the following recommendations: For Australian Pavilion Proposals Align your event with Australia s climate priorities. Demonstrate clear relevance to COP31 themes. Highlight collaboration and innovation. Explain the expected outcomes and audience engagement. Ensure the proposal complements Australia s Pavilion programme. For Travel Support Applications Clearly demonstrate how attending COP31 will contribute to your work or community. Explain your organization s climate-related activities or experience. Address every assessment criterion thoroughly. Provide complete supporting documentation. Submit your application before the deadline. Read more HERE Important Dates Activity Deadline COP31 Climate Conference Travel Support Program 27 July 2026 Australian Pavilion Event Proposal Submission 31 July 2026 COP31 Conference 9–20 November 2026 How to Apply Interested applicants should: Determine which opportunity best matches their interests. Carefully review the eligibility requirements. Prepare all required information and supporting documents. Submit their application through the relevant official application process before the deadline. Consult the official guidance documents if additional clarification is needed. Applicants seeking travel support should review the official Grant Opportunity Guidelines before preparing their submission. Why You Should Apply Australia s participation in COP31 presents a unique opportunity for organizations and individuals to help shape the country s contribution to one of the world s most important climate conferences. Whether by organizing events within the Australian Pavilion or participating through the Travel Support Program, successful applicants will contribute to international discussions on climate policy, renewable energy, sustainability, and implementation of the Paris Agreement. These opportunities also provide valuable exposure, networking, and collaboration with governments, researchers, industry leaders, civil society organizations, and climate experts from around the world. Conclusion The Australian Government s COP31 engagement opportunities offer an exceptional chance for organizations, young people, First Nations communities, researchers, businesses, and climate leaders to become active participants in global climate action. With Australia serving as President of Negotiations at COP31, these initiatives aim to showcase the country s leadership, innovation, and commitment to achieving meaningful climate outcomes. Prospective applicants are encouraged to review the official guidance carefully, prepare strong submissions, and apply before the respective deadlines. Whether contributing through an Australian Pavilion event or attending the conference with travel support, participants will have the opportunity to help shape Australia s presence at one of the most significant international climate events of the decade. title: SPARK Change-Makers Project 2026 in Ghana: Capacity Building, Mentorship and Grant Funding for Youth-Led Organizations
summary: Youth-led organizations and social enterprises in Ghana now have an exciting opportunity to strengthen their impact through the SPARK Change-Makers Project 2026 , an initiative under the EU-Ghana Youth Empowerment Programme . Implemented by SOS Children s Villages Ghana in partnership with the European Union and other collaborating organizations, the programme is designed to empower youth-led and youth-focused civil society organizations (CSOs) and social enterprises with the knowledge, skills, mentorship, and technical support they need to expand their impact and contribute to sustainable development. Beyond training and organizational development, the programme also provides successful participants with the opportunity to compete for grant funding , enabling them to scale their projects and create lasting change within their communities. If your organization is passionate about empowering young people, promoting innovation, protecting the environment, or strengthening communities, this programme offers an excellent platform to build capacity and access future funding opportunities. About the SPARK Change-Makers Project The SPARK Change-Makers Project is a comprehensive organizational development programme that seeks to strengthen youth-led organizations before providing access to financial support. Rather than offering immediate grants, the programme adopts a phased approach , ensuring participating organizations first receive intensive capacity-building support to improve their effectiveness, governance, sustainability, and long-term impact. Selected organizations will participate in a structured programme that equips them with practical skills, strategic guidance, and expert support before becoming eligible to compete for grant funding. The initiative focuses on empowering young people between the ages of 18 and 35 by strengthening the organizations that serve and represent them. Programme Benefits Organizations selected for the SPARK Change-Makers Project will receive a wide range of support designed to strengthen their operations and increase their impact. Benefits include: Comprehensive organizational capacity development. Leadership and organizational skills training. One-on-one coaching from experienced professionals. Expert mentorship throughout the programme. Technical assistance and advisory support. Networking opportunities with partners and stakeholders. Access to future grant funding opportunities. Support for developing sustainable income-generating activities. Improved organizational sustainability and long-term growth. Increased ability to deliver impactful community projects. Opportunity to Access Grant Funding One of the major highlights of the SPARK Change-Makers Project is the opportunity to access grant funding. Organizations that successfully complete the programme s capacity-strengthening phase become eligible to compete for grants that can help expand and scale their initiatives. This ensures that participating organizations are better prepared to manage funding effectively while maximizing community impact. Who Can Apply? The programme is open to organizations that are youth-led or youth-focused and operate within the programme s six implementing regions of Ghana. Eligible applicants include: Youth-led civil society organizations (CSOs). Youth-focused civil society organizations. Youth-led social enterprises. Youth-focused social enterprises. Priority Focus Areas The SPARK Change-Makers Project supports organizations working in sectors that contribute to youth empowerment and sustainable development. Priority areas include: 1. Gender Empowerment Projects that promote gender equality, women s empowerment, inclusion, and equal opportunities for young people. 2. Agriculture and Environmental Protection Initiatives focused on sustainable agriculture, climate resilience, environmental conservation, food security, and green innovation. 3. Peace and Security Projects that promote peaceful communities, conflict prevention, civic participation, and social cohesion. 4. Culture and Creative Industries Organizations supporting arts, culture, creative entrepreneurship, and cultural preservation among young people. 5. Digital Technology and Innovation Projects leveraging digital tools, technology, innovation, and entrepreneurship to improve lives and create opportunities for youth. Selection Criteria Applications will be evaluated based on several important factors. Strong applications should demonstrate: Youth leadership within the organization. Clear organizational goals and objectives. Active community engagement. Commitment to inclusion and diversity. Environmental sustainability. Potential for long-term impact. Organizational readiness for growth and scaling. Organizations that clearly demonstrate these qualities will be well positioned during the selection process. Why This Programme Matters Many youth-led organizations possess innovative ideas and strong community connections but often face challenges such as limited organizational capacity, inadequate technical support, and restricted access to funding. The SPARK Change-Makers Project addresses these challenges by investing in organizational development before providing access to financial resources. Participants gain practical knowledge, professional guidance, and stronger institutional capacity, enabling them to build sustainable organizations that continue delivering impact long after the programme ends. Important Deadline Application Deadline: 31 July 2026 Interested organizations should complete and submit their applications before the deadline to be considered for the programme. Click here for more and to apply Why You Should Apply If your organization is committed to creating positive change for young people in Ghana, the SPARK Change-Makers Project offers far more than funding. It provides the skills, mentorship, networks, and institutional support needed to build a stronger, more sustainable organization capable of delivering lasting impact. Whether you work in gender equality, environmental sustainability, peacebuilding, the creative economy, or digital innovation, this programme can help you strengthen your organization while positioning it for future grant opportunities.
